title: How your site looks when it is shared
checkLabels.p5: The line for the phone is there
checkLabels.p6: The description for Google is there
checkLabels.t7: It is all in the head
checkLabels.t9: The image has a full address
why.empty: The editor is empty. The task is to ADD four lines, not delete the page. Bring the code back.
why.untouched: You have not touched the code. Your link is still a bare blue strip.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
why.lost-content: You deleted something instead of adding. The heading, the sentence, the charset, the tab title, the line for the phone, the description — all of it stays. You are only adding four lines to the head.
hint2.lost-content: Compare with the original code. Nothing was supposed to disappear.
why.no-og-title: There is no title for sharing. Without it the social network guesses — sometimes it takes the <title>, sometimes something else.
hint2.no-og-title: A <meta> tag again. But with property, not name. A small difference everyone gets wrong at least once.
hint3.no-og-title: <meta property="og:something" content="the value"> — property says which one it is, content says what.
why.no-og-desc: There is no description for sharing. A bare link with no explanation gets no clicks.
why.no-og-image: There is no image. This is the most important of the four. A link without a picture is a thin grey strip the eye skips over. A link with one is half a screen.
why.no-og-url: There is no address. Without it the network does not know the page's official address — and the same page can show up under three different links.
why.outside-head: The tags are there, but not in the head. <meta> goes in <head>. In the body it does nothing — the program reading your site only looks at the head.
hint2.outside-head: Put them next to charset and viewport. All meta tags live together.
why.empty-content: One or more of the tags is empty. An empty content is the same as a missing tag — it says nothing.
hint2.empty-content: Go through the four. Which one has empty quotes?
why.relative-image: The image is there, but its address is not a full one. Facebook is not on your site — it has nothing to measure a relative path from.
hint2.relative-image: bike.jpg works in an because the browser is already on your page. Here it is not.
hint3.relative-image: The address must start with https:// and lead to the image if you paste it into a browser yourself.
blocks.0.text: Someone liked your site and sends it to a friend. They copy the address and paste it into a chat.
blocks.1.text: And what appears? A bare blue link. A thin strip of text nobody will click.
blocks.2.text: Now remember what it looks like when someone shares an article from a big site: a large image, a title, a sentence beneath. It takes up half the screen and makes you look.
blocks.4.text: It is not an accident. Nor is it Facebook's doing — it simply reads what the site told it.
blocks.5.text: More <meta> tags in the head. This time following a standard called Open Graph — invented by Facebook, but read today by everything: Viber, WhatsApp, LinkedIn, Slack, Telegram, Discord, iMessage. Even X, which has a format of its own, falls back on these tags when the others are missing.
blocks.7.text: Notice: property, not name. A small difference everyone gets wrong at least once. And all four go in the head — in the body they do nothing.
blocks.9.text: Of the four, og:image is the one that makes the difference. A link without an image is a thin grey strip. A link with one is half a screen.
blocks.10.text: Its address must be FULL — with https:// and everything. A relative path will not work. Facebook is not on your site, it has nothing to measure from.
blocks.11.text: The size: around 1200 by 630 pixels. Anything else gets cropped.
blocks.13.text: You will ask: I already have a <title> and a description. Why write the same thing again?
blocks.14.text: Because it does not have to be the same. <title> is for Google and for the tab. og:title is for the person seeing the link in a chat.
blocks.15.text: Google wants keywords. The friend in the chat wants a reason to click. Sometimes those are different sentences.
blocks.16.text: One is written for a search engine. The other for a person who just received a message.
blocks.18.text: Do not guess. There is a way to see exactly what will appear.
blocks.19.text: Facebook has the Sharing Debugger. LinkedIn has the Post Inspector. You paste the address and see the card. X used to have its own tool, but took it down — there is no official way to check in advance there.
blocks.20.text: The simplest test works everywhere, though: send the link to yourself. Open a chat with yourself in Viber, WhatsApp or Telegram, paste the address and watch what comes out.
blocks.21.text: And a warning: these networks cache. You change the image and Facebook still shows the old one. That is what the tools are for — with them you make it read the page again.
blocks.22.text: Your turn. Make your site look good when someone shares it.
blocks.23.text: This is the last invisible thing in this course. And probably the cheapest: four lines that decide whether anyone clicks at all.
